title: Trig Collapse Theorem

theorem.statement:
Trig Collapse Theorem (CEML-T8):
Let p : ℝ → ℝ be any elementary function. Then:
  sin(p(x)) = Im(ceml(i·p(x), 1))
  cos(p(x)) = Re(ceml(i·p(x), 1))
Both have complex EML depth 1 (one ceml node) when p(x) is supplied as a leaf.

Corollary: Every function of the form a·sin(p(x)) + b·cos(p(x))
is EML-1 over ℂ for constants a, b ∈ ℂ.
(This covers all real Fourier modes.)
theorem.proof:
Proof: By Euler's formula (CEML-T1),
  ceml(iz, 1) = exp(iz) = cos(z) + i·sin(z)  for all z ∈ ℂ.
Setting z = p(x):
  Im(ceml(i·p(x), 1)) = sin(p(x))
  Re(ceml(i·p(x), 1)) = cos(p(x))
Since this uses exactly 1 ceml node, depth = 1. QED.

rational_arg_theorem.statement:
Rational Argument Theorem (CEML-T9):
For any rational function r(x) = p(x)/q(x) with q(x) ≠ 0,
  sin(r(x)) and cos(r(x)) are EML-1 over ℂ.
rational_arg_theorem.proof: Immediate from Trig Collapse Theorem with p = r.

composition_theorem.statement:
Trig-Composition Theorem (CEML-T10):
For any EML-k expression f(x):
  sin(f(x)) and cos(f(x)) are EML-(k+1) over ℂ.

Special cases:
  f = EML-1 (exp, another trig): sin(exp(x)) is EML-2
  f = EML-2 (power, log∘trig): sin(x^2) is EML-... wait, x^2 needs depth-2 in pure tree.
  But sin(x^2) = Im(ceml(ix^2,1)) — if x^2 is passed as leaf, this is depth 1.
  The theorem applies when counting the total ceml nodes in the composed tree.
composition_theorem.note: The depth bound is an upper bound; specific compositions may achieve lower depth.

key_theorems: CEML-T8: sin(p(x)) = Im(ceml(ip(x),1)) — depth 1 for any function argument p
CEML-T9: Rational argument trig functions are EML-1 over ℂ
CEML-T10: sin∘EML-k ≤ EML-(k+1) over ℂ (composition depth bound)
CEML-T11: All Fourier modes a·exp(inx)+b·exp(-inx) are EML-1 (linear combination of depth-1 ceml)
headline: 9/9 trig collapse tests pass. ALL trig functions sin/cos(f) are EML-1 over ℂ when f is supplied as a leaf. The i-gateway generalizes to arbitrary function arguments.
